Egypt & Nubia

Three thousand years along the Nile.
← All regions

Pharaonic Egypt and its southern rival Kush left monuments and portraits of startling precision. The pyramids alone span a thousand years — and Nubia built more of them than Egypt did.

Click any image to enlarge. Use ← → or close with Esc.
Great Pyramid of Giza
Old Kingdom · c. 2560 BCE
Tomb of Khufu. 2.3 million stone blocks, averaging 2.5 tons each. The tallest human-made structure on earth for 3,800 years.
Great Sphinx of Giza
Old Kingdom · c. 2500 BCE
Carved from a single limestone outcrop, the oldest known monumental sculpture. Its nose was already missing before Napoleon arrived.
Narmer Palette
Early Dynastic · c. 3100 BCE
A ceremonial siltstone palette showing a king smiting an enemy — one of the earliest monuments of unified Egypt, with its conventions already fully formed.
Bust of Nefertiti
Amarna period · c. 1345 BCE
Painted limestone, found in the workshop of the sculptor Thutmose at Amarna. Still in Berlin; Egypt has asked for it back for a century.
Mask of Tutankhamun
New Kingdom · c. 1323 BCE
Eleven kilograms of solid gold, inlaid with lapis, carnelian, and obsidian. The funerary mask of a boy-king who died at nineteen.
Karnak Temple Complex
New Kingdom onward · 2000 BCE – 100 CE
A city of temples at Thebes, built and rebuilt by thirty dynasties. The hypostyle hall alone has 134 columns.
Temple of Hatshepsut
New Kingdom · c. 1470 BCE · Deir el-Bahari
Three stacked colonnaded terraces built into a cliff for Egypt's longest-reigning female pharaoh. Her successor tried to erase her — and failed.
Tomb of Nefertari
19th dynasty · c. 1255 BCE · Valley of the Queens
Painted for Ramesses II's favorite wife — the queen walks through the underworld, plays the board game Senet, and greets the gods. Colors still vivid.
Abu Simbel
Reign of Ramesses II · c. 1264 BCE
Four colossal statues guard the temple front. In the 1960s the whole complex was cut apart and moved 200 feet up to escape Lake Nasser.
Rosetta Stone
Ptolemaic · 196 BCE
A decree written three times — in hieroglyphs, Demotic, and Greek — that became the key to reading Egyptian. Champollion cracked it in 1822.
Pyramids of Meroë
Kingdom of Kush · 300 BCE – 300 CE · Sudan
Over 200 pyramids, steeper and smaller than Egypt's, built for Nubian royalty. Sudan has more pyramids than Egypt — about twice as many.
Book of the Dead
New Kingdom onward · 1550 BCE – 50 BCE
Spells written on papyrus to guide the deceased through the Duat. The Weighing of the Heart against a feather is its most famous scene.
Colossi of Memnon
New Kingdom · c. 1350 BCE · Luxor
Twin seated statues of Amenhotep III, sixty feet tall. After an earthquake cracked one, it "sang" at dawn for two centuries — until Romans repaired it.
Fayum Mummy Portraits
Roman Egypt · 1st–3rd c. CE
Encaustic panels placed over the faces of mummies. The most personal, most realistic portraits to survive from the ancient world — they still look you in the eye.
Temple of Dendur
Roman Egypt · 15 BCE
A small sandstone temple given to the United States in 1965 when Lake Nasser was about to drown it. Reassembled stone by stone inside the Met.
